issue.message: Composable functions that return Unit should start with an uppercase letter
issue.explanation: @Composable functions without a return type should use similar naming to classes, starting with an uppercase letter and ending with a noun. @Composable functions with a return type should be treated as normal Kotlin functions, starting with a lowercase letter.

issue.message: Modifier parameter should be the first optional parameter
issue.summary: Guidelines for Modifier parameters in a Composable function
issue.explanation: The first (or only) Modifier parameter in a Composable function should follow the following rules: - Be named `modifier` - Have a type of `Modifier` - Either have no default value, or have a default value of `Modifier` - If optional, be the first optional parameter in the parameter list

issue.message: Modifier parameter should be the first optional parameter
issue.summary: Guidelines for Modifier parameters in a Composable function
issue.explanation: The first (or only) Modifier parameter in a Composable function should follow the following rules: - Be named `modifier` - Have a type of `Modifier` - Either have no default value, or have a default value of `Modifier` - If optional, be the first optional parameter in the parameter list

issue.message: Modifier parameter should be the first optional parameter
issue.summary: Guidelines for Modifier parameters in a Composable function
issue.explanation: The first (or only) Modifier parameter in a Composable function should follow the following rules: - Be named `modifier` - Have a type of `Modifier` - Either have no default value, or have a default value of `Modifier` - If optional, be the first optional parameter in the parameter list

issue.message: Modifier parameter should be the first optional parameter
issue.summary: Guidelines for Modifier parameters in a Composable function
issue.explanation: The first (or only) Modifier parameter in a Composable function should follow the following rules: - Be named `modifier` - Have a type of `Modifier` - Either have no default value, or have a default value of `Modifier` - If optional, be the first optional parameter in the parameter list

issue.message: Modifier parameter should be the first optional parameter
issue.summary: Guidelines for Modifier parameters in a Composable function
issue.explanation: The first (or only) Modifier parameter in a Composable function should follow the following rules: - Be named `modifier` - Have a type of `Modifier` - Either have no default value, or have a default value of `Modifier` - If optional, be the first optional parameter in the parameter list

issue.message: Modifier parameter should be the first optional parameter
issue.summary: Guidelines for Modifier parameters in a Composable function
issue.explanation: The first (or only) Modifier parameter in a Composable function should follow the following rules: - Be named `modifier` - Have a type of `Modifier` - Either have no default value, or have a default value of `Modifier` - If optional, be the first optional parameter in the parameter list

issue.summary: Modifier.composed should only be used for modifiers that invoke @Composable functions
issue.explanation: `Modifier.composed` allows invoking @Composable functions when creating a `Modifier` instance - for example, using `remember` to have instance-specific state, allowing the same `Modifier` object to be safely used in multiple places. Using `Modifier.composed` without calling any @Composable functions inside is unnecessary, and since the Modifier is no longer skippable, this can cause a lot of extra work inside the composed body, leading to worse performance.

issue.summary: Modifier.composed should only be used for modifiers that invoke @Composable functions
issue.explanation: `Modifier.composed` allows invoking @Composable functions when creating a `Modifier` instance - for example, using `remember` to have instance-specific state, allowing the same `Modifier` object to be safely used in multiple places. Using `Modifier.composed` without calling any @Composable functions inside is unnecessary, and since the Modifier is no longer skippable, this can cause a lot of extra work inside the composed body, leading to worse performance.

issue.summary: `State<T>` will autobox values assigned to this state. Use a specialized state type instead.
issue.explanation: Calling `mutableStateOf<T>()` when `T` is either backed by a primitive type on the JVM or is a value class results in a state implementation that requires all state values to be boxed. This usually causes an additional allocation for each state write, and adds some additional work to auto-unbox values when reading the value of the state. Instead, prefer to use a specialized primitive state implementation for `Int`, `Long`, `Float`, and `Double` when the state does not need to track null values and does not override the default `SnapshotMutationPolicy`. See `mutableIntStateOf()`, `mutableLongStateOf()`, `mutableFloatStateOf()`, and `mutableDoubleStateOf()` for more information.

issue.summary: `State<T>` will autobox values assigned to this state. Use a specialized state type instead.
issue.explanation: Calling `mutableStateOf<T>()` when `T` is either backed by a primitive type on the JVM or is a value class results in a state implementation that requires all state values to be boxed. This usually causes an additional allocation for each state write, and adds some additional work to auto-unbox values when reading the value of the state. Instead, prefer to use a specialized primitive state implementation for `Int`, `Long`, `Float`, and `Double` when the state does not need to track null values and does not override the default `SnapshotMutationPolicy`. See `mutableIntStateOf()`, `mutableLongStateOf()`, `mutableFloatStateOf()`, and `mutableDoubleStateOf()` for more information.

This usually causes an additional allocation for each state write, and adds some additional work to auto-unbox values when reading the value of the state. Instead, prefer to use a specialized primitive state implementation for `Int`, `Long`, `Float`, and `Double` when the state does not need to track null values and does not override the default `SnapshotMutationPolicy`. See `mutableIntStateOf()`, `mutableLongStateOf()`, `mutableFloatStateOf()`, and `mutableDoubleStateOf()` for more information.

issue.explanation: Calling `mutableStateOf<T>()` when `T` is either backed by a primitive type on the JVM or is a value class results in a state implementation that requires all state values to be boxed. This usually causes an additional allocation for each state write, and adds some additional work to auto-unbox values when reading the value of the state. Instead, prefer to use a specialized primitive state implementation for `Int`, `Long`, `Float`, and `Double` when the state does not need to track null values and does not override the default `SnapshotMutationPolicy`. See `mutableIntStateOf()`, `mutableLongStateOf()`, `mutableFloatStateOf()`, and `mutableDoubleStateOf()` for more information.

issue.summary: `State<T>` will autobox values assigned to this state. Use a specialized state type instead.
issue.explanation: Calling `mutableStateOf<T>()` when `T` is either backed by a primitive type on the JVM or is a value class results in a state implementation that requires all state values to be boxed. This usually causes an additional allocation for each state write, and adds some additional work to auto-unbox values when reading the value of the state. Instead, prefer to use a specialized primitive state implementation for `Int`, `Long`, `Float`, and `Double` when the state does not need to track null values and does not override the default `SnapshotMutationPolicy`. See `mutableIntStateOf()`, `mutableLongStateOf()`, `mutableFloatStateOf()`, and `mutableDoubleStateOf()` for more information.
